region_of_origin: Switzerland (Zurich); widely served in Singapore health cafés
food_subgroup: Overnight-soaked rolled oats with Greek yoghurt, grated apple, honey, and toasted nuts; served as a breakfast bowl (~250g)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Bircher Muesli or overnight-soaked oat muesli. Closest parent would be a generic muesli or oat-based breakfast food concept.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bircher muesli / overnight-soaked oat muesli with yoghurt and fruit
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A00GF is the closest FoodEx2 parent for muesli-type preparations; no exact code for Bircher Muesli with yoghurt and apple exists in the standard hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Bircher Muesli as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250g (one bowl), comprising roughly 60g rolled oats, 80g Greek yoghurt, 60g grated apple, and apple juice absorbed during overnight soaking, topped with toasted nuts and honey.

nutrient_source.title: Bircher Muesli — USDA SR Legacy component calculation (rolled oats + Greek yoghurt + apple + honey + almonds)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation using USDA SR Legacy entries for rolled oats (FDC ID 173904), plain Greek yoghurt (FDC ID 170903), raw apple (FDC ID 171688), honey (FDC ID 169640), and dry-roasted almonds (FDC ID 170567), weighted to a 250g bowl composition. Per-100g values scaled from the composite bowl calculation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of cold-soaked muesli and oat-based breakfast preparations — University of Sydney GI Database reference for muesli (not heat-treated)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of ~50 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entries for cold-soaked muesli and overnight oat preparations. Cold soaking promotes resistant starch formation and the malic acid from apple juice further moderates glucose response, supporting a GI in the low-to-moderate range (~45–55). GL per 250g serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (50 × 47g) / 100 ≈ 23.5. Confidence: 0.9 (referenced analogous estimate).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~32mg/100g; ~80mg per 250g bowl) with beneficial potassium and calcium from Greek yoghurt and oats
hypertension.guidance: Bircher Muesli is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its sodium content is low (~80mg per bowl), and the calcium from Greek yoghurt (~163mg per bowl) and oat beta-glucan provide modest antihypertensive and LDL-lowering benefits consistent with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~50 (low) and GL ~23.5 per bowl; moderate sugar load (~22g per bowl) from apple juice, honey, and grated apple partially offset by high protein and oat beta-glucan
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bircher Muesli has a low GI (~50) and is preferable to granola or hot porridge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the glycaemic load per bowl (~23.5) is moderate and the ~22g sugar per serving warrants portion awareness, particularly for those on insulin or sulfonylureas — the whey protein from Greek yoghurt stimulates GLP-1 and insulin secretion, meaningfully attenuating the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Oat beta-glucan (soluble fibre) with LDL-lowering evidence; low saturated fat (~0.7g/100g); beneficial nuts providing unsaturated fats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bircher Muesli is cardioprotective: oat beta-glucan (≥3g/day threshold achievable with a 250g bowl) has FDA-approved heart-health claims for LDL reduction, saturated fat is low at ~1.75g per bowl, and toasted nuts contribute cardioprotective monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fatty acids — making this an appropriate breakfast choice for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ingredients: oat beta-glucan (prebiotic), Greek yoghurt (probiotic), apple quercetin, and nut omega-3 ALA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bircher Muesli has a high anti-inflammatory dietary profile suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; oat beta-glucan supports gut microbiome diversity, Greek yoghurt provides probiotic Lactobacillus strains that modulate the gut-immune axis, and apple quercetin and nut-derived ALA contribute anti-inflammatory activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bircher Muesli is a Swiss-origin cold-preparation breakfast dish invented around 1900 by Swiss physician Maximilian Oskar Bircher-Benner at his Zurich sanatorium as part of a raw-food therapeutic diet. The original recipe used raw rolled oats soaked overnight in water with sweetened condensed milk, lemon juice, grated raw apple, and crushed hazelnuts. Modern Singapore café versions — served at venues such as Common Man Coffee Roasters, Grain Traders, The Butcher's Wife, and hotel breakfast buffets — use apple juice for overnight soaking, Greek yoghurt in place of condensed milk, honey as sweetener, and varied toasted nuts (almonds or walnuts). The texture is soft, creamy, and slightly chewy, with a naturally sweet and tangy flavour from the apple and yoghurt. Per 250g bowl: approximately 340 kcal, 10g protein, 8g fat, 52g carbohydrate, 22g sugar, 5g dietary fibre, and ~80mg sodium. GI is approximately 50 (low), with a glycaemic load of ~23.5 per bowl — lower than hot oatmeal due to resistant starch formation during cold soaking and the moderating effect of apple malic acid. Nutritionally, Bircher Muesli is superior to dry granola for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its higher protein content (from Greek yoghurt whey), lower GI, and the GLP-1-stimulating effect of dairy protein. Oat beta-glucan provides soluble fibre with FDA-recognised LDL-lowering properties relevant to cardiovascular disease management. Sodium is low, making it appropriate for hypertension. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable. Key nutrients include calcium (65mg/100g), potassium (150mg/100g), magnesium (28mg/100g), phosphorus (130mg/100g), and B-vitamins from oats and yoghurt. Source: USDA SR Legacy component calculation.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by USDA SR Legacy component calculation across five ingredient categories (rolled oats, Greek yoghurt, apple, honey, almonds) weighted to a 250g bowl. No single approved-source entry exists for Bircher Muesli as a composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database cold-soaked muesli references. Overall confidence is medium (0.7) consistent with the input record.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite Bircher Muesli preparation is not directly available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database; individual component fatty acid data exists but composite proportioning introduces uncertainty beyond the scope of this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content of the composite soaked preparation is not reported as a single value in USDA SR Legacy for this dish type. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for the component ingredients at typical preparation quantities). No core macro is zero: protein_g = 4.0g, fat_g = 3.2g, carbohydrate_g = 20.8g — all non-zero and nutritionally consistent with the ingredient composition. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database cold-soaked muesli references; GL per 250g bowl = (50 × 47g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 23.5. FoodOn URI is null: no specific FoodOn concept exists for Bircher Muesli; closest_parent match noted. SNOMED CT code is null: no SNOMED CT food concept identified for this dish. FoodEx2 code A00GF used as closest_parent for muesli-type preparations.
